sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 81, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 80 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 2.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the green frame is now INSIDE the dark frame on the right panel! The green frame with white center is now inside the dark frame. This looks like the goal state - the green frame is positioned inside the dark frame, matching the pattern where the left panel has a dark frame (with the green frame outside) and the right panel has a dark frame (with the green frame inside).
Wait, but the level is not completed yet (level_completed is false). Let me check the segmentation to see if anything else changed.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the green frame is inside the dark frame on the right panel. The dark frame on the left panel is empty (no green frame inside). Maybe the goal is to have the green frame inside BOTH dark frames?
But I can only control one green frame at a time (the right one, after the left one was "activated"). Or maybe I need to move the left green frame inside its dark frame as well.
Let me check the segmentation to see the current state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green id={n['id']} boundary={n['boundary']} children={n['children']}")
    if n['color'] == 'c' and 10 < n['pixels'] < 50:
        print(f"Dark id={n['id']} boundary={n['boundary']} children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see:
- Green id=7: boundary=[[27, 45], [27, 47], [29, 47], [29, 45]] - rows 27-29, cols 45-47, inside dark frame id=6
- Dark id=6: children=[7] - the dark frame contains the green frame
- Green id=2: boundary=[[21, 18], [21, 20], [23, 20], [23, 18]] - left panel, top, still at the same position
- Dark id=9: boundary=[[32, 11], [32, 15], [36, 15], [36, 11]] - left panel, dark frame, children=[10] (contains white center)
So the green frame is now inside the dark frame on the right panel. The dark frame on the left panel is empty (no green frame inside, just the white center).
The level is not completed yet. Maybe I need to also move the left green frame inside its dark frame?
The left green frame is at rows 21-23, cols 18-20. The left dark frame is at rows 32-36, cols 11-15. The interior of the left dark frame is rows 33-35, cols 12-14.
To move the left green frame to the interior of the left dark frame:
- DOWN: 33 - 21 = 12 rows = 4 actions (each DOWN moves 3 rows)
- LEFT: 18 - 12 = 6 cols = 2 actions (each LEFT moves 3 cols)
But wait, earlier I thought the actions control the right green frame, not the left one. Let me check if the actions now control the left green frame again.
Actually, looking at the history, the actions have been controlling the right green frame since I moved the left green frame to the top-left corner. Maybe I need to "activate" the left green frame again to control it.
Or maybe the goal is just to have the right green frame inside the right dark frame, and the level should be completed. But the level_completed flag is false.
Let me try moving the left green frame to see if the actions now control it again.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the segmentation. The green frame id=2 is at rows 21-23, cols 18-20, with children=[5] (the white center). The dark frame id=9 is at rows 32-36, cols 11-15, with children=[10] (the white center).
So the left dark frame has a white center (id=10), and the left green frame has a white center (id=5). The right dark frame has the green frame id=7 inside it, and the green frame id=7 has a white center (id=8).
Hmm, the left dark frame doesn't contain the left green frame. Maybe I need to move the left green frame inside the left dark frame.
But how do I control the left green frame? The actions have been controlling the right green frame. Maybe I need to click on the left green frame to "select" it again.
Let me try clicking on the left green frame (rows 21-23, cols 18-20, center at row 22, col 19).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the left green frame to select it
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 19}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
